and artistic outlets in the late twentieth century. It has inspired two 
directors to adapt it for the screen. In 1992, director Andrey 
Svislotskiy of Russia's Pilot Animation Studio made an eight-minute 
animated short film entitled  Гипнэротомахия (Gypnerotomahiya), 
where the rather grotesque figure of Poliphilo journeys through a 
barren, grey land full of obelisks depicting melting figures making 
love, blood dripping and other nightmarish themes, searching for the 
portal that leads him to Polia’s tower. The next half of the film 
shows sleeping Polia having nightmares of...

monster seemed to recoil in fear, two that were behind made bold enough to

golden columns that supported silver doves with tails and wings spread out. 
The third, held by a group of grotesquely attenuated satyrs, was centred with 
a thin pipe hung with masks and roses and capped with children's heads.

... of this rare citation."0 The 
narrative locale delimited by seen "Horix" (D 26v) and remembered 
"Sorix" (D 31r) is the interior of the pyramid temple-a 
space of some 
complexity that will serve as a portal via which we may enter the entire 
work." 
On entering, Poliphilus is fascinated by the grotesque architectural 
decorations that seem aesthetically generated from the unnatural 
mythic copulations depicted in the hallway mosaics (D 27r-28r). The 
darkness of the way ahead-presumably 
signifying the dangers of his 
concupiscence-causes 
Poliphilus to retreat, but a terrifying dragon...

... out from "hoggish calamitie" (D 30r) 
to the "cleere Sunne" (D 31r). So ends the first stage of Poliphilus's train- 
ing in wisdom. His own untrained affections make him susceptible to 
devilish entrapment troped via classical myths of illicit lust leading to 
monster generation, fascinating grotesque-work, and labyrinthine im- 
prisonment. He has been baptized into the strife between good and bad 
desire, between grace and damnation, and between wisdom and lust, 
all three of which he is yet to deal with more fully. 
The reader might ask: having gone through the pyramid of the...

... Indalomena (Phantasia) ushers him into the second room 
of "discourses and reason marueilously wouen"; Mnemosyna (Mem- 
ory) brings him to the Queen herself ([D 49r])."8 Poliphilus describes the 
extraordinary inner court with its "Chesse-boord" pavement (D 49v), 
iconographic wall schemes, grotesque decorations, and topiary (D 5or- 
51r). The "imperiall Maiestie" and richly bejeweled attire of the Queen 
(D 51v-52r) align her with late-sixteenth-century English royal portrai- 
ture, and Dallington reveals his own aesthetic competence by sliding in 
amongst the list of her chambers a...

... to be drawn upon, drawn out from, and
used for constructing new work.”45 Similarly, the various tropes
used by Colonna to cause awe and wonderment both in
Poliphilo and in the reader derive from elementary principles
of mnemonics: surprise and strangeness (like the oxymoron, for
example, and the grotesque in art), exaggeration, orderliness
and pattern, copiousness, opposition, and contrast.46 All of
these were among the main features of the ornaments used
by the monks for making mnemonically powerful associations.
And these ornaments were usually playful and startling, for
mnemonic...
